meta.methodology: 3x7 Adversarial Voting + Evidence Tier Classification (A+ to D)
meta.disclaimer: Research synthesis for educational purposes only. Not professional security, legal, or medical advice.

evidenceTiers.A+: Randomized controlled trial or replicated operational cohort with measured outcomes
evidenceTiers.A: Peer-reviewed study or large institutional dataset with quantified results
evidenceTiers.B: Quasi-experimental, transparent observational study (n>100), replicable methodology
evidenceTiers.C: Limited peer review, single-site, or industry white paper
evidenceTiers.D: Anecdotal, marketing claims, or untested cooperative drills

crimeStatistics.metric: Victims who knew attacker (violent crime, NCVS 1982-84)
crimeStatistics.source: BJS NCVS — Violent Crime by Strangers and Nonstrangers
crimeStatistics.note: 46% strangers for rape/robbery/assault aggregate; varies by crime type

crimeStatistics.metric: Aggravated assaults involving firearms, knives, or other weapons

crimeStatistics.note: Figure widely cited; exact year and offense mix should be verified per report

crimeStatistics.metric: Sexual assaults committed by multiple attackers

dataGap.missingMetric: Percentage of people who detected probable multi-attacker scenario unfolding and completely avoided via premeditated strategy
dataGap.explanation: Victimization surveys measure incidents that occurred, not near-misses avoided. Proactive avoidance is structurally under-counted in crime statistics.
proxyMetrics: EAAA risk assessment skill improvement (24-month follow-up)
EAAA reduced time to detect acquaintance danger (Assess unit)
NCVS verbal/passive resistance → 57% lower injury odds
Street fight multi-attacker: 22% reactive escape vs 11% defeat

eaaa.source: NEJM 2015 RCT; Eur J Psychotraumatol 2023 real-world implementation

cooperColorCode.name: Cooper Color Code (White/Yellow/Orange/Red)
cooperColorCode.origin: Lt. Col. Jeff Cooper, combat pistol instruction
cooperColorCode.evidenceStatus: Widely adopted (military, LE, aviation); limited outcome RCTs linking color-code training to victimization reduction

stayAlertStayAlive.name: Stay Alert, Stay Alive (military personal security)
stayAlertStayAlive.evidenceStatus: Institutional adoption; operational reports; no civilian RCT

oop.name: OODA Loop (Observe-Orient-Decide-Act)
oop.relevance: Decision-speed framework; faster threat recognition → earlier avoidance window

multiAttacker.recommendation: Escape is 2x more successful than fighting when outnumbered (22% vs 11%)

ncvsSelfProtection.source: Bachman et al. 2022 — NCVS assault victims 65+
ncvsSelfProtection.note: Verbal/passive resistance before/during injury vs no resistance or physical only

stsFramework.description: Strategy (why/win condition) → Tactic (when/how) → Technique System (trained skill bundle) → Individual Techniques

strategies.name: Avoidance-First
strategies.winCondition: Zero victimization — never enter the fight
strategies.plainEnglish: Spot danger early and leave before anyone throws a punch.

strategies.name: Vigilance & Premeditated Exit
strategies.winCondition: Near-miss avoided — no injury, no legal exposure
strategies.plainEnglish: Stay switched on, plan routes, leave at the first wrong feeling.

strategies.name: Escape-Priority
strategies.plainEnglish: If already in trouble, break contact and run. Winning the fight is not the goal.

strategies.name: Standup Finish (1v1)
strategies.winCondition: Incapacitate standing opponent in first 10 seconds
strategies.plainEnglish: Simple boxing, create distance, do not go to the ground.
strategies.evidence: 64% KOs in first 10 sec street fights; boxing force data

strategies.name: Control & Restraint
strategies.plainEnglish: Take down, hold position, cuff or contain — not knock out.
strategies.evidence: Marietta/St. Paul BJJ DT: 37–59% less force, fewer injuries

strategies.name: Combat Dominance
strategies.winCondition: Win the engagement — sport or consensual fight
strategies.plainEnglish: Train to beat an opponent who agrees to fight you under rules.
strategies.evidence: MMA/UFC outcome data; weakest on street avoidance

strategies.name: Scenario Scripting
strategies.plainEnglish: Practice specific what-if scenarios (knife, gun, multiple attackers).
strategies.evidence: Krav Maga — limited peer review; high school variance

tactics.name: Risk Assessment (Assess)
tactics.description: Read social cues, isolation attempts, boundary testing from known persons

tactics.name: Forceful Verbal Boundary
tactics.description: Clear no, name the behavior, do not soften to be polite

tactics.name: Cooper Yellow→Orange→Red
tactics.description: Escalate mental alertness as anomalies stack

tactics.name: Route & Exit Preplanning
tactics.description: Know exits before entering venues; park for drive-out

tactics.name: Multi-Party Group Detection
tactics.description: Spot flanking, bait-and-ambush, friend groups coordinating

tactics.name: Premeditated Exit
tactics.description: Leave before engagement — the missing metric we wish we could measure

tactics.name: Create Distance
tactics.description: Push kick, shove, angle off — break clinch

tactics.name: Strike Burst (Jab-Cross-Hook)
tactics.description: First 10 seconds decide most standing fights

tactics.name: Sprawl / Anti-Takedown
tactics.description: Stay standing on concrete; deny ground game

tactics.name: Level Change → Double-Leg
tactics.description: Most common MMA takedown; LE control entry

tactics.name: Positional Control (No GNP)
tactics.description: Side control, mount for restraint — minimize trauma

tactics.name: Ground Finish (Sport)
tactics.description: RNC, mount GNP — UFC-proven; street risk high

tactics.name: Stand-Up Recovery
tactics.description: Only 41% return to feet once down — prioritize getting up

techniqueSystems.name: EAAA / Empowerment Self-Defense
techniqueSystems.techniqueFocus: Boundary setting, wrist releases, forceful verbal resistance

techniqueSystems.name: Wrestling Control System
techniqueSystems.techniqueFocus: Takedowns and top control without submissions

techniqueSystems.name: BJJ Law Enforcement / Control
techniqueSystems.techniqueFocus: Control with measured force — validated PD outcomes

techniqueSystems.name: BJJ Sport / Guard Game
techniqueSystems.techniqueFocus: Win on ground in ruleset — poor street multi-attacker fit

techniqueSystems.name: Integrated MMA Stack
techniqueSystems.techniqueFocus: All ranges — sport validated, avoidance not trained

techniqueSystems.name: Escape-First Physical Stack
techniqueSystems.techniqueFocus: Minimal entanglement — get off the X

techniqueSystems.name: Krav Maga / Tactical SD
techniqueSystems.techniqueFocus: Broad scenarios — evidence thin, school quality varies

techniqueSystems.name: Traditional MA (Low-Contact)
techniqueSystems.techniqueFocus: Confidence and fitness — poor combat transfer

minimalSystem.name: AAA Exit Protocol
minimalSystem.tagline: The simplest system any human can learn — minutes per day, not years in a gym
minimalSystem.evidenceBasis: EAAA medical trial (NEJM 2015), NCVS resistance outcomes, Louie Martin street-fight cohort (n=154)

compoundLogic.effect: Highest leverage — zero injury risk from that encounter
Multiplies odds before contact — structurally under-measured but logically dominant
compoundLogic.evidence: Premeditated exit tactic (A+); proactive metric is a known data gap

compoundLogic.effect: Doubles success vs fighting when outnumbered
Marginal gain only after first three layers fail
compoundLogic.evidence: 64% KOs in first 10 sec — but only if you are still there to throw them

dailyRoutine.action: Yellow mindset: Who is near me? Am I being isolated or flanked? Is someone I know testing boundaries?
dailyRoutine.plainEnglish: Look up from your phone. Notice patterns, not just faces.

dailyRoutine.action: When your gut tightens, say internally: 'This is real. I don't need permission to leave.'
dailyRoutine.plainEnglish: Stop negotiating with the feeling that something is off.

weeklyRoutine.action: Pick one real place you go (bar, parking lot, date). Walk through leaving before contact.
weeklyRoutine.plainEnglish: Mental practice counts. Athletes do it; you can too.

weeklyRoutine.action: At one familiar location, identify two exits and where you'd go for people or light.
weeklyRoutine.plainEnglish: You already do this on airplanes. Do it where you actually live.

weeklyRoutine.action: Say out loud: 'Stop. No. I'm leaving.' Until your voice is firm, not apologetic.
weeklyRoutine.plainEnglish: Forceful words reduced injury odds 57% in victim surveys — no gym required.

weeklyRoutine.action: 30-second jog or fast walk. Escape is a physical skill for some bodies.
weeklyRoutine.plainEnglish: Only if you can. Leaving still works walking.

ifContact.action: Create distance — push, angle off, don't clinch
Run toward people, light, or open space — not deeper into the alley
Only if trapped: palm heel or push kick, then run

optionalPhysical.source: EAAA Act unit (17% of full 12-hour program)
skills: Get to your feet if knocked down
optionalPhysical.note: Add only after AAA habits are automatic — not instead of them

minimalSystem.nhiVerdict: After scoring seven strategies, fifteen tactics, eleven technique systems, and twenty-two individual techniques across three disagreeing reviewers, one conclusion survived every argument: the highest-return skill is not a punch or a choke — it is leaving before the fight exists. The AAA Exit Protocol distills the only approach with medical-trial proof (EAAA) into three daily habits and twenty minutes per week. Any human, any age, any fitness level can start today. Martial arts compound on top; they do not replace this.
minimalSystem.honestLimits: No system guarantees survival. Guns, surprise, and size disparity can overwhelm any training. This protocol shifts odds using the strongest evidence we have — it does not make you invincible. Proactive 'I left before it started' is still not scientifically measured; we flag that gap openly.

defensivePosture.description: How you stand, move, and present your body before and during contact — and what changes when a weapon appears
defensivePosture.principle: Posture exists to protect your brain and vitals, keep your feet mobile, and preserve exit options. It is not a fighting pose for cameras.
defensivePosture.evidenceBasis: Boxing guard biomechanics, wrestling stance literature, NCVS resistance outcomes, FBI/BJS weapon involvement statistics, LE armed-encounter doctrine (no civilian disarm RCTs)

stance.name: Exit-Ready Stance
stance.plainEnglish: Stand like you might need to run in any direction — not like a UFC weigh-in.
stance.feet: Shoulder-width or slightly wider. Lead foot (strong-side back) 12–18 inches ahead. Toes angled 30–45° — not squared like a sport fighter.
stance.weight: 60% on balls of feet, knees soft (not locked). Heels light — you can pivot or sprint without shifting first.
stance.hips: Low enough to stay balanced on uneven ground; high enough to run. Think 'athletic ready,' not deep wrestling shot stance.
stance.orientation: Slightly bladed (45°) — presents narrower target, lead shoulder closer to threat, rear hand loaded.
errors: Wide MMA square (exposes gut, can't run)

footwork.name: Distance & Angle Footwork
footwork.plainEnglish: Move your feet to create space and exit lanes — never trade punches in place.

rules.why: Straight retreat lets them chase faster; angling opens escape routes
Cross-step = tangled legs = fall on concrete
Rear foot drives forward past lead foot — fastest path to distance
Sport footwork looks good; street needs meters not inches

priorityMoves: Sprawl only if grabbed — then stand immediately

handsArms.name: Compact Guard — Hands High, Elbows In
handsArms.plainEnglish: Protect your jaw and ribs without waving your hands like you're asking a question.
handsArms.handPosition: Both hands at cheekbone/temple height. Lead hand slightly forward (6–8 inches from chin). Rear hand glued to jaw.
handsArms.elbows: Tucked against floating ribs — not flared like a bodybuilder. Elbows in blocks hooks to the body.
handsArms.forearms: Angle 45° — catches straight shots on forearms if unavoidable; don't reach out to 'block.'
handsArms.openVsClosed: Street default: partially open palms (palms-out = de-escalation signal + ready palm-heel). Fists only if contact is imminent.
handsArms.framing: If grabbed: forearms form a frame against chest/throat — create space, don't pull them closer.
errors: Hands at waist (telegraphs, slow to head)
Reaching to parry (gets you pulled into clinch)

headVitals.name: Chin Down, Vitals Hidden
headVitals.plainEnglish: Your brain is the target. Tuck your chin and don't present your throat, solar plexus, or groin.
headVitals.chin: Tucked — chin touches collarbone area. One clean shot to an untucked chin ends most fights.
headVitals.headPosition: Eyes on threat's chest/shoulders (see hands without telegraphing fear). Peripheral vision for flanks.
headVitals.shoulders: Slightly hunched — shrinks target, protects carotid. Not rigid — stay loose to move.
headVitals.torso: Slight forward lean from ankles (not waist) — harder to push over, faster first step.
headVitals.groin: Hips neutral — not leaning back exposing groin for kicks. Lead leg slightly turned in.
headVitals.throat: Never chin-up posturing. Verbal de-escalation with chin up = invitation.

weaponResponses.prevalence: 73.7% of U.S. murders involve firearms; 9% of NCVS violent victimizations (2023)
weaponResponses.defaultRule: Comply and exit — do not attempt disarm

whenDisplayed.action: Freeze sudden movement. Hands visible, palms open at chest height.
Verbal compliance: 'Okay. You can have it. Nobody needs to get hurt.'
whenDisplayed.why: De-escalation buys seconds; NCVS shows verbal resistance reduces injury when no shot fired yet

whenDisplayed.action: Give demanded property slowly. Move items, not your whole body toward them.
whenDisplayed.why: Robbery motive = property; comply to end transaction

whenDisplayed.action: Create distance only when they disengage or look away — then run to cover/crowd.
whenDisplayed.why: Exit when window opens — don't test if they're still aiming

whenDisplayed.action: If they move you to secondary location (car, back room): reassess — fighting may become last resort.
whenDisplayed.why: LE doctrine: secondary location dramatically increases lethality

doNot: Attempt a Hollywood disarm (no peer-reviewed civilian success rate)
Reach inside clothing quickly (reads as drawing)
Stare at the barrel — watch their hands and eyes
Charge a drawn gun at close range (reaction time favors shooter inside ~21 ft)
Assume it is fake — treat every presentation as real until you are safe
weaponResponses.evidence: FBI UCR homicide data; BJS NCVS 2023; LE armed robbery protocols; Krav disarm drills rated Tier D (no outcome RCTs)

weaponResponses.prevalence: Part of 27.6% aggravated assaults involving weapons (FBI aggregate)
weaponResponses.defaultRule: Distance is survival — do not clinch a knife

whenDisplayed.action: Increase distance immediately — push-step back, object between you.
whenDisplayed.why: Knife wins clinch range; you will be cut getting 'control'

whenDisplayed.action: Hands up, palms out — 'Stay back!' while moving.
whenDisplayed.why: Guard up protects slash lines to neck/face

whenDisplayed.action: Angle offline — don't stand in front of their power hand.
Throw or toss available object (bag, chair) — not as fight finisher, as exit buy.
whenDisplayed.why: Moment of flinch creates run window

whenDisplayed.action: Run toward people/light. Accept you may be cut — keep moving.
whenDisplayed.why: Fatal bleeds take time; distance stops repeated thrusts

doNot: Attempt arm-trap disarms without years of live training
Go to ground — ground + knife = worst case
Focus on blocking one strike (knives attack in series)
Turn and run blindly (trip = death — run with awareness)
weaponResponses.evidence: MacYoung/Thompson edged-weapon analysis; street footage; sport knife sparring shows cut accumulation; disarm drills Tier C–D for civilians

weaponResponses.defaultRule: Close distance or create distance — middle range is worst

whenDisplayed.why: You cannot out-block a full swing

whenDisplayed.action: If close: crash in with forearm shield, clinch, exit.
whenDisplayed.why: Inside swing arc reduces power — but high risk

whenDisplayed.action: Protect head at all costs — turn shoulder, guard high.
whenDisplayed.why: Blunt trauma to skull is fight-ender

weaponResponses.evidence: Observational fight analysis; boxing headgear studies on blunt trauma

whenDisplayed.action: Same compliance protocol as visible firearm.
whenDisplayed.why: You cannot verify — betting wrong is fatal

whenDisplayed.action: Comply, exit, report. Let police verify.
whenDisplayed.why: Property replaceable; confirmation impossible in moment

weaponResponses.evidence: LE encounter doctrine; robbery completion statistics

defensivePosture.nhiSummary: Stance, footwork, guard, and vitals presentation are the physical layer beneath AAA Exit. You do not need a perfect boxing stance — you need feet that can run, hands that protect your jaw, a chin that is tucked, and the discipline to never square up for a duel when a weapon is involved. When a gun or knife appears, the evidence unanimously favors compliance and distance over disarm fantasies. Krav Maga weapon drills score Tier C–D because nobody has measured civilian disarm success in peer-reviewed outcomes. Your job is to go home — not to win Instagram.

fightScience.description: Hard numbers on force, knockouts, reaction time, range, ground probability, and fight duration — the physics behind what works
fightScience.evidenceBasis: Walilko et al. Olympic boxer head acceleration; Archives of Budo punch force; Boxing Science amateur biomechanics; Louie Martin n=154; LAPD ground study; Tueller 1983; OODA/Boyd; ESPN Fightnomics
fightScience.nhiSummary: Fight science says three things civilians need to know: (1) most fights are decided in the first 10 seconds — 64% of street knockouts happen there; (2) force in a lab and force that ends fights are different problems — karate and boxing hit similar newtons, but only boxers prove delivery under resistance; (3) action beats reaction at close range, which is why avoidance beats fighting and why squared-up duels favor the person who moved first. The brain shuts off from rapid head acceleration — hooks and falls rotate the head worse than straight shots. Ground is common (62–73%) but getting back up is not (41%). Train for burst output and immediate exit, not round 3 cardio.

kineticChain.plainEnglish: A knockout punch is not an arm punch — it is a whole-body whip from floor to fist.

sequence.contribution: Ground reaction force — push off back foot to start chain
~40–50% of punch impulse in trained boxers (pelvis drives fist speed)
Transfers hip torque; 'sit down' on cross adds mass behind punch
Final acceleration — short path = less telegraph
Peak force 1,800–2,700N lab measured; jaw target = shortest path to brainstem

kineticChain.formula: Impulse = mass × Δvelocity — effective mass (whole body behind fist) matters more than arm strength

forceData.source: Combat sports kick force literature (~2.5–3.5kN range)
Point-scoring mechanics — retracted before impact mass develops

knockoutMechanics.plainEnglish: Knockouts are a brainstem/off-switch event from rapid head acceleration — not 'hurt enough'.

linearAcceleration.description: Linear head acceleration (straight shots to jaw) — Wayne State tolerance curve range cited in combat sports TBI literature

rotationalAcceleration.description: Rotational acceleration (hooks, uppercuts, falls) — stronger KO correlation in boxing TBI reviews

targets.mechanism: Rotational + linear — chin shot rotates head, shears brainstem
Vagal / pain shutdown — not true KO but fight-stop
Pain + watering — rarely true KO, buys time

reactionTime.plainEnglish: Whoever acts first at close range often wins — your brain cannot defend what it has not processed yet.

metrics.context: Which hand? Block or move? — street attacks are choice problems
Observe-Orient-Decide-Act under surprise — defender always behind
Average person covers 21 ft before average draw+fire — not a magic distance

reactionTime.implication: Inside arm's reach, the attacker who moves first lands before you can process, chamber, and deliver a trained response — unless you were already acting (leaving, hands up, pre-committed exit).

zones.name: Escape range
zones.science: Energy cost to chase scales with distance² perception

zones.name: Kick range
zones.science: Leg reach exceeds arm; teep creates 1–2m impulse gap

zones.name: Punch range
zones.science: 64% KOs in first 10 sec at this range in street footage

zones.name: Clinch range
zones.dominant: Elbows, head position, frames — knife danger zone
zones.science: Tueller: edged weapon lethal inside ~21 ft if you freeze

zones.name: Ground
zones.dominant: Wrestling/BJJ — 62–73% end here but 41% stand back up
zones.science: Concrete removes grappling safety; gravity adds impact G-force

groundScience.mythDebunked: 90% of fights go to the ground
groundScience.concreteMultiplier: Falls add 50–150G linear impacts — rotational falls from throws exceed many standing shots

buckets.outcome: Rare — crowd breakup, weapon, or both gassed

fightDuration.energyNote: Anaerobic alactic burst ~7–10 sec; glycolytic ~30–90 sec — street fights rarely need 'cardio', they need explosive exit

ufcVsStreet.note: Sport finishes are rule-bound; street has no ref, no tap, weapons, third parties

keyPrinciples.title: Delivery > Force
keyPrinciples.text: Similar lab newtons between karate and boxing — only boxing proves the chain under live resistance.

keyPrinciples.title: First 10 Seconds Decide
keyPrinciples.text: 64% of street KOs land before most people finish their first technique — burst and exit.

keyPrinciples.title: Action Beats Reaction
keyPrinciples.text: Choice reaction 400–600ms+ — defender inside punching range is always behind.

keyPrinciples.title: Rotation Knocks Out
keyPrinciples.text: Hooks and falls cause rotational brain acceleration — worse KO correlation than linear jabs in TBI reviews.

keyPrinciples.title: Ground Is a Trap
keyPrinciples.text: 73% hit ground; only 41% stand back up — sport ground game ≠ concrete survival.

keyPrinciples.title: Mass Behind the Shot
keyPrinciples.text: Effective mass (whole body) > arm strength — sit-down cross vs arm-only snap punch.

victimFitnessResearch.description: What research actually says about victim physical capacity and surviving violent encounters

dataGap.missingMetric: Victim fitness level (cardiovascular fitness, strength, BMI, training history) measured at time of assault and correlated with survival, escape, or injury-free outcome
dataGap.explanation: NCVS and most crime surveys do not ask victims about gym fitness, VO2 max, sport background, or physical training hours. Studies measure resistance behaviors and injury after the fact — not pre-incident fitness.
dataGap.whatWouldBeNeeded: Prospective cohort linking fitness assessments to incident outcomes, or NCVS supplement with validated physical capacity measures
proxyMetrics: Disability status (ambulatory, cognitive, self-care) as inverse capacity proxy — BJS NCVS 2017-2019
Age 65+ injury and resistance outcomes — Bachman et al. 2022 NCVS
Escape vs defeat when outnumbered — Louie Martin n=154 street fights
Self-protective behavior type (run vs physical fight) — Tark & Kleck 2004, Guerette & Santana 2010 NCVS
EAAA 2-hour physical unit + assault reduction — not a fitness trial but closest RCT with physical component

disabilityProxy.source: BJS Crime Against Persons with Disabilities 2009-2019 (Harrell 2021)
disabilityProxy.shareOfViolentCrime: Persons with disabilities = 12% of population but 26% of nonfatal violent crime victims

byDisabilityType.note: Highest rate — decision/processing speed, not gym fitness
Closest proxy for mobility/flee capacity — still 3.2× general population

disabilityProxy.plainEnglish: People with mobility limitations are victimized far more often. This is not proof that marathon runners survive fights better — but it strongly suggests physical capacity to detect, avoid, and flee matters.
disabilityProxy.caveat: Disability increases targeting and vulnerability through many pathways (routine activities, dependence on caregivers, cognitive load) — not only 'can't run fast.'

ageAndCapacity.source: Bachman, Stenger & Sharo 2022; NCVS elderly injury literature

findings.finding: Assault victims 65+ sustain physical injury in 16.2% of incidents (NCVS)
findings.implication: Baseline injury rate for older adults in assaults

findings.finding: Verbal/passive resistance only → 57% lower injury odds vs no resistance or physical resistance
findings.implication: For older victims, fighting back physically may not be the optimal survival strategy

findings.finding: Assaults by family members → 150%+ higher injury likelihood vs strangers for 65+
findings.implication: Fitness matters less than relationship context for this demographic

findings.finding: Older victims more likely to need medical care and suffer serious injury vs younger (NCVS/NIBRS literature)
findings.implication: Age proxies declining capacity — frailty beats gym strength in many encounters

escapeAndMobility.source: Louie Martin Martial Journal n=154; NCVS self-protective behaviors

outnumbered.note: Running/escaping when already outnumbered succeeds 2× more than standing fight — requires mobility, not bodybuilding

runningAway.rapeAvoidance: Generally associated with rape avoidance across NCVS studies
runningAway.fitnessLink: Inferred B-tier: sprint and agility help but never directly measured against VO2 max in victims

returnToStanding.fitnessLink: Inferred B-tier: lower-body power and get-up drills help 59% who stay down — strength-specific

resistanceOutcomes.source: Tark & Kleck 2004; Guerette & Santana 2010; Wong & Balemba 2016 meta-analysis

completion.effect: Significantly more likely to avoid rape completion
completion.injuryNote: Injury often precedes resistance when sequence controlled

completion.effect: Strongest rape-avoidance; 93% ↓ robbery completion odds under opportunity model (Guerette 2010)
completion.injuryNote: Struggling/attacking without weapon linked to injury in some robbery types

completion.effect: Effective nonforceful physical — among best predictors in acquaintance rape samples
completion.injuryNote: Lower injury risk than prolonged physical duel

completion.effect: 57% less injury — best for 65+ per Bachman 2022

resistanceOutcomes.injurySequence: Ullman, Tark & Kleck, Hollander: when temporal order is controlled, resistance does not increase injury — victims resist because they are already being hurt
resistanceOutcomes.plainEnglish: Fitness helps most when the winning move is running, standing up once, or a 10-second burst — not a 3-minute ground war.

eaaaPhysicalNote.notMeasured: VO2 max, grip strength, or body composition changes
eaaaPhysicalNote.plainEnglish: The only gold-standard trial with a physical component reduced assaults dramatically — but the program is mostly cognitive/behavioral. Two hours of physical technique is not a fitness regimen.

fitnessInferenceMatrix.survivalLink: High for multi-attacker and knife scenarios
fitnessInferenceMatrix.data: 22% escape when outnumbered; running = top nonforceful resistance

fitnessInferenceMatrix.survivalLink: High — 59% fail to return to feet once down

fitnessInferenceMatrix.data: Anaerobic alactic window matches fight duration science
Most street fights end or go indecisive in under 60 sec; no NCVS fitness link
Effective mass helps strikes; no victim BMI→survival RCT

victimFitnessResearch.nhiSummary: Nobody has run the study you want: fit gym people vs sedentary people in real violent encounters. NCVS does not record victim fitness. What we have are proxies: disabled and mobility-impaired people are victimized 3–4× more; running beats fighting when outnumbered 2:1; older adults reduce injury 57% with verbal/passive resistance, not brawling; EAAA's 2 hours of physical work inside a brain-first program cut assaults in half. Inference: train minimum effective fitness for escape (sprint, get-up, burst) after AAA habits — not bodybuilder cardio for a fight that should never happen.
victimFitnessResearch.honestLimits: Correlation is not causation. Disability victimization reflects targeting, social isolation, and care dependence — not only inability to fight. Being fit does not substitute for avoidance. Firearms and surprise negate fitness advantages.

firstPrinciples.method: First principles thinking: strip every inherited belief (belts, UFC 1993, gym slogans, movie choreography) down to irreducible truths — physics, biology, crime statistics, game theory — then rebuild only what logically follows. If a conclusion cannot be derived from an axiom plus evidence, it does not rank on this site.
firstPrinciples.tagline: Don't ask 'what did my instructor teach?' Ask 'what must be true for a human to survive predatory violence?'

axioms.axiom: The civilian win condition is non-victimization — not defeating an opponent
axioms.firstPrinciples: In sport, win = referee stoppage or submission. In predatory violence, win = you leave intact without being assaulted. Different games, different optima.
axioms.destroys: Defaulting to MMA/BJJ gym culture as primary self-defense

axioms.axiom: Violence is asymmetric — weapons, numbers, and surprise negate technique
axioms.firstPrinciples: ~74% of murders involve firearms; ~48% juvenile incidents involve multiple offenders; predation selects vulnerability. Fair fights are rare.
axioms.destroys: Training for 1v1 unarmed duel as the default scenario

axioms.axiom: Leverage is temporal — the highest-return moment is before contact
axioms.firstPrinciples: Every second earlier you detect and exit removes the entire injury distribution. Post-contact options are strictly worse in expected value.
axioms.destroys: Spending years on ground game before teaching exit habits

axioms.axiom: Human reaction time guarantees the initiator advantage at close range
axioms.firstPrinciples: Choice reaction 400–600ms+; punches land in <200ms. Defender who waits to process loses unless already acting (hands up, leaving).
axioms.destroys: Cold-start chambered techniques as reliable defense

axioms.axiom: Knockouts are physics — rapid head acceleration shuts off the brainstem
axioms.firstPrinciples: Not pain tolerance. Jaw/chin shots, hooks, and falls rotate the brain. First 10 seconds decide most standing encounters.
axioms.destroys: Point-karate touch scoring as street preparation

axioms.axiom: Claims without measured outcomes are hypotheses — not recommendations
axioms.firstPrinciples: Evidence tiers: RCT > government data > observation > doctrine > marketing. Unmeasured must be labeled, not assumed.
axioms.destroys: Krav 'street lethal' without victimization RCTs; 90% ground myth

axioms.axiom: Training time is finite — optimize expected survival value per hour
axioms.firstPrinciples: 12-hour EAAA moved assault rates 50–57%. Years of sport BJJ have no equivalent civilian victimization trial. Marginal hours matter.
axioms.destroys: 10-year belt journeys before addressing acquaintance predation

axioms.axiom: Threat geometry dictates response — gun compliance ≠ knife clinch
axioms.firstPrinciples: Firearm at close range: reaction favors shooter — comply and exit. Knife: distance and run. One-size 'fight back' violates physics and data.

derivedPrinciples.principle: Avoidance-first is the rational default strategy
derivedPrinciples.evidence: EAAA RCT Tier A+; only medical-trial victimization reduction

derivedPrinciples.principle: If contact is unavoidable: 10-second standup burst, then exit
derivedPrinciples.evidence: 64% KOs in first 10 sec; boxing kinetic chain under resistance

derivedPrinciples.principle: Ground is high-risk environment on concrete — stand up fast

Weapon presented = compliance and distance, not disarm drills

Karate and boxing similar lab newtons — delivery under resistance differs
No NCVS fitness field; mobility disability 3–4× victimization — escape fitness inferred only
65+ verbal/passive resistance 57% less injury; guns require compliance

rebuildOrder: Map threat reality (A2) → weapons, groups, surprise
Allocate training time to highest leverage phase (A3, A7) → pre-contact
Validate only with measured outcomes (A6) → adversarial 3×7 filter
Add physical layer only where derived (A4, A5) → burst, escape, posture
firstPrinciples.nhiSummary: First principles destroys the martial arts industry's default stack. You do not start with 'which art is best?' You start with: What is winning? (leaving unharmed.) What is the threat? (unfair, often armed, often known.) When is leverage highest? (before contact.) What proof exists? (RCTs and NCVS, not demos.) Everything on this site — EAAA first, AAA daily habits, escape over ground fighting, weapon compliance — is rebuilt from those truths. Tradition is consulted only where evidence confirms it (boxing delivery, wrestling sprawls). Everything else is ranked or rejected.

karateNhiAnalysis.title: Historical Okinawan Karate vs Resilience Evidence — AI NHI Reconciliation
karateNhiAnalysis.executiveSummary: The Okinawan masters' original intent aligns surprisingly well with Resilience first principles and top findings — avoidance first, stun-and-escape, close-range tools, low kicks, no first attack. Where modern karate often fails is implementation drift: point sparring, compliant bunkai, and vital-point folklore without pressure testing. Historical philosophy scores high; low-contact modern traditional MA scored last (rank 7) in 3×7 adversarial voting. Kyokushin and bunkai-focused full-contact training partially recover what sport karate lost.

historicalContext.plainEnglish: Okinawa's peasant-class history produced karate as concealed, decisive, escape-oriented fighting — not trophies. That original design matches our data. Japan-era sportification and global McDojo compliance drills are a different product sharing the same name.
historicalContext.note: Historical narrative — not independently quantified in NCVS/RCT

masterAnalysis.coreTeaching: Karate ni sente nashi (no first attack); character; kata with vital strikes/throws
aligns: A1 civilian win = avoid engagement
A3 pre-contact leverage
Funakoshi's avoidance mirrors EAAA/AAA philosophy
tensions: Modern Shotokan often emphasizes long-range kihon and sport kata over his written de-escalation emphasis
No RCT links Shotokan training to reduced victimization
masterAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Funakoshi's philosophy is Tier A+ compatible with avoidance-first. Most Shotokan gyms today teach his techniques without his exit mindset.

masterAnalysis.coreTeaching: Street-proven fighter; face first; simultaneous block-counter; Naihanchi/Tekki; one-knuckle; centerline
aligns: A4 action-reaction — simultaneous counter beats sequential block-then-punch
Close-range palm/elbow/forearm matches our guard and clinch data
D3 ten-second burst mindset
tensions: Motobu's era anecdotes are Tier C/D as outcome data
Simultaneous counter still loses to pre-contact exit when available
masterAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Motobu is the historical karateka closest to fight-science contact principles. His method assumes you are already in range — Resilience still prefers you never get there.

masterAnalysis.coreTeaching: Hard/soft; close power; Bubishi 48 techniques; vital points; grappling
aligns: Palm, elbow, knee close-range toolkit matches top SD technique lists
Hard/soft = frame/create distance then strike (escape-stack)
Bubishi grappling parallels wrestling/LE control concepts
tensions: Kyusho/vital-point pressure tactics lack peer-reviewed street outcome RCTs (Tier C-D)
Bubishi bunkai without live resistance = traditional MA rank 7 problem
masterAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Goju's curriculum content is well-designed for chaos IF trained with Kyokushin-level pressure. The Bubishi is a technique library — not proof.

masterAnalysis.coreTeaching: Full-contact conditioning; ikken hissatsu; low kicks; body hardening; goshin-jutsu throws
masterAnalysis.resilienceAlignment: HIGH for physical layer / LOW for avoidance
aligns: C6 stress transfer — full-contact is what Resilience demands for technique validation
Low kicks match calf-kick/teep evidence (Tier A)
Ikken hissatsu = first 10 sec KO science (64% street KOs)
Conditioning supports escape-burst fitness inference
tensions: Kyokushin culture rewards standing trade — weak on A3 avoidance
No victimization RCT for Kyokushin practitioners
Body hardening ≠ gun or multi-attacker solution
masterAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Oyama solved the delivery problem Motobu cared about. Best historical karate path for contact-phase evidence — still secondary to AAA/EAAA brain work.

principleReconciliation.evidence: A1, A3, EAAA A+, Funakoshi sente nashi
Jaw/temple/groin align with knockout mechanics; pressure-point mythology unvalidated
Elbow Tier A score 4; palm heel streetSafety 4; front knee Tier B

Kinetic chain science; gyaku-zuki 2645N — delivery under resistance is the gap
Valid IF pressure-tested (Abernathy/Enkamp method); invalid if compliant two-step
principleReconciliation.tier: B if tested, D if compliant

principleReconciliation.evidence: C6 adversarial criterion; Kyokushin full-contact; traditional low-contact ranked last

techniqueReconciliation.verdict: CONFIRMED — in AAA optional burst and escape-stack
CONFIRMED at close range — rotational KO risk to you and them
CONFIRMED in clinch — then exit, not Muay Thai plum exchange
CONFIRMED — Oyama low kick aligns with Muay/MMA street data

CONFIRMED concept — boxing delivers it under live resistance better

driftProblem.title: Why modern karate often scores Tier D while Okinawan intent scores Tier B+
drifts: Point sparring rewards snap contact — trains opposite of effective mass (snap punch 600N vs cross 2500N)
Compliant bunkai partners — fails C6 stress transfer criterion
Kata as performance — not scenario + resistance
Belt timeline economics — violates A7 finite training time vs EAAA 12 hours
Kyusho seminars — Tier D without outcomes
Missing Funakoshi's sente nashi in competitive culture
driftProblem.recoveryPath: Kyokushin or bunkai-focused dojo (Enkamp/Abernathy/McCarthy) + explicit escape drills + Funakoshi/Oyama philosophy read alongside EAAA-style awareness

order.why: Palm, elbow, knee, low kick, forearm — if trapped
Bubishi content valid only under C6 testing
Same hip physics, better live-proof chain

karateNhiAnalysis.nhiFinalVerdict: You are largely correct about what the Okinawan masters intended — and Resilience data vindicates that intent more than it vindicates the average modern karate school. Funakoshi's 'no first attack' is avoidance-first (A+ logic). Motobu's simultaneous close-range violence is fight-science contact phase. Oyama's full-contact low kicks and ikken hissatsu match the 10-second KO window and Tier A kicking data. Miyagi's Bubishi is a sound chaos toolkit IF pressure-tested. The fracture is historical: original karate was escape-oriented peasant self-defense; exported sport karate became point dancing. Our adversarial vote ranked 'Traditional MA (low-contact)' last not because karate history is wrong — but because most gyms no longer train what Motobu and Oyama actually did. Train like Oyama, think like Funakoshi, bunkai like Abernathy, measure like EAAA — and the traditions reconcile with the numbers.
karateNhiAnalysis.honestLimits: Master biographies and Bubishi claims are Tier C historical sources. No study isolates 'Okinawan-style karate' vs 'boxing' for civilian victimization. Kyusho effectiveness remains unsubstantiated in peer review. This analysis reconciles philosophy and technique overlap — it does not elevate karate above EAAA on outcomes without an RCT.

onePunchOneKick.title: One Punch + One Kick — Tomoyose / Egami / Ikken Hissatsu vs Evidence

sources.quote: In the old days, karate focused on one punch and one kick. People have gotten away from this philosophy.

sources.teaching: Concentrated on choku-zuki/gyaku-zuki and mae-geri for rapid soldier training

straightPunch.plainEnglish: Physics supports the punch. Our data supports the delivery system when trained against resistance — boxing cross ranks #1; karate gyaku-zuki ranks #5 with same lab newtons but unproven street transfer.

frontKick.resilienceEquivalent: Teep push kick / front kick to groin-knee
frontKick.tier: A for escape teep; B for groin thrust
frontKick.plainEnglish: Tomoyose's one kick maps best to mae-geri kekomi (groin/solar plexus) for stun and teep-style kekomi for push-away escape. Calf kick outranks both for sport proof, but front kick is faster to deploy — matches your 'quickest kick' claim.

combos.mapsTo: D3 ten-second burst; first 10 sec decides 64% KOs
combos.exitRequired: Run after combo — not stand and admire

combos.sequence: Straight punch distraction → front kick push-away
combos.mapsTo: AAA contact protocol step 4; teep creates distance

minimalWeaponsStack.oneSentence: Tomoyose's two weapons are the contact-layer capsule — not the whole system. Funakoshi's avoidance is still layer zero.

whyTheseTwo.plainEnglish: With proper kime, hip drive, alignment, and targeting, straight punch + front kick cover most contact-phase scenarios. Fancy kata techniques are refinements of these two kinetic chains.
straightPunch: Fastest high-power hand strike — full body weight through centerline
Targets face, throat, solar plexus, ribs at close or medium range
Chains easily; base for palm-heel, forearm, elbow at closer range
frontKick: Quickest kick — minimal chamber, low grab risk vs high kicks
Distance control: groin/knee/shin stun or teep push-away escape
Keage snap or kekomi thrust — both map to burst-and-exit logic

drillProgression.title: 8-Week Minimal Weapons Progression
drillProgression.prerequisite: AAA awareness habits running before heavy contact work

drills: Slow mae-geri kekomi to pad: groin line + solar plexus height — 3×20 each leg
Teep push on heavy bag: full hip extension, recover to guard, no pause — 3×15
Shadow: kick → immediate lateral step toward exit lane — 5×1 min rounds
phases.exitTest: 10 full-power teeps in 30 sec with guard recovery; verbal 'RUN' cue after each

drills: Makiwara or bag: gyaku-zuki with hip rotation — 5×50 (Egami volume principle)
Cross on bag from boxing guard: chin down, exhale on impact — 3×30
Partner pad: single punch only — attacker feeds range, you one-shot and reset
phases.exitTest: 20 clean crosses on bag in 60 sec; partner feels snap without telegraph

drills: Combo A: mae-geri groin → gyaku-zuki face → sprint 10 m (Tomoyose chain)
Combo B: feint punch → teep push → create 3 m gap → run
Ippon kumite: attacker commits one committed strike; you answer with one combo then exit
phases.exitTest: Land combo on resisting pad holder in under 2 sec; exit before third beat

drills: Motobu feed: partner throws committed punch — simultaneous block-counter with gyaku-zuki
Grab → front kick shin/knee while stripping grip (Bubishi entry)
Multi-angle: attacker from 45° — offline step + one weapon + run
phases.exitTest: Survive 3 surprise attacks in 60 sec with ≤2 techniques each + successful exit

drillProgression.maintenance: 20 min/week scenario + daily 3 min awareness. Re-test combos monthly under fatigue.

bubishiMotobuCounters.title: Bubishi / Motobu Counters — Mapped to One Punch + One Kick
bubishiMotobuCounters.principle: Motobu taught simultaneous defense-and-attack (same beat). Bubishi flows are technique libraries — valid only under live feed (C6). Each counter collapses to punch, kick, or close-range variant, then exit.

counters.motobuBubishi: Kake-uke or nagashi-uke offline + gyaku-zuki same beat
counters.resilienceFit: STRONG — 64% KOs in first 10 sec; one-beat counter

counters.motobuBubishi: Rotate offline; strike with back-fist or straight punch to jaw
counters.minimalWeapon: Oi-zuki or gyaku-zuki after angle step

counters.motobuBubishi: Bubishi nami-gaeshi / leg sweep or kick to knee while stripping
counters.minimalWeapon: Mae-geri kekomi to knee/shin + pull away
counters.resilienceFit: STRONG — kick faster than elaborate lock

counters.motobuBubishi: Bubishi wedge + knee; Motobu favored low kick from clinch
counters.resilienceFit: STRONG for escape; not ground fight

counters.motobuBubishi: Step inside arc; straight punch down centerline before hook lands
counters.minimalWeapon: Choku-zuki / gyaku-zuki to solar plexus or jaw
counters.resilienceFit: STRONG — straight beats wide arc on timing

counters.motobuBubishi: Sprawl or knee; Bubishi grappling entries
counters.minimalWeapon: Front kick to face/chest (stop) or knee if already low
counters.resilienceFit: PARTIAL — prefer run if seen early; kick is late counter

bubishiMotobuCounters.trainingNote: Drill each counter as ippon kumite: one feed, one minimal response, mandatory 10 m sprint. Add resistance in weeks 7–8. Do not chain into kata sequences — ikken hissatsu then exit.

onePunchOneKick.nhiVerdict: The 'one punch, one kick' philosophy is one of the strongest historical alignments with Resilience fight science. Tomoyose and Egami independently converged on depth over breadth (first principle A7), ikken hissatsu matches the 64% first-10-second KO window, and mae-geri → gyaku-zuki is a legitimate burst-and-exit combo. Resilience adds three corrections: (1) the straight punch must be trained with boxing-level delivery, not point-karate snap; (2) the front kick's highest-value street role is push-away escape (teep logic), not a Hollywood groin-only finisher; (3) awareness still outranks both — Egami's soldiers needed fast skills, but civilians need EAAA-style detection more. Reject the '90% of situations' marketing fraction unless someone measures it. Accept the Okinawan minimalism as the correct contact-phase curriculum after AAA habits exist.
onePunchOneKick.honestLimits: Tomoyose quote is oral tradition (Tier C). Egami soldier training has no victimization RCT. Makiwara excellence does not automatically transfer to adrenalized unknown attackers without C6 testing.

boxingNhiAnalysis.title: Boxing Legends vs Resilience Evidence — AI NHI Reconciliation
boxingNhiAnalysis.executiveSummary: Boxing legends — Tyson, Cus D'Amato, Dempsey, Ali — converge on what Resilience fight science already proves: mastered basics under live pressure, explosive first-beat disruption, head movement against telegraphed swings, and finish-fast mentality. Boxing + Muay Thai standup ranks Tier A in adversarial voting (#2–3 depending on stack) because sparring validates delivery (C6). Where legend lore overreaches: 'dominate most street encounters' is unmeasured; forward swarming (peek-a-boo) trades well in 1v1 but conflicts with multi-attacker and weapon exit logic; boxing assumes stand-up with no kicks, ground, or guns. Resilience verdict: boxing is the best-evidenced contact-phase delivery system for civilians who must fight standing — but only after AAA/EAAA avoidance fails, and best paired with karate low kicks and explicit sprint exits.

historicalContext.plainEnglish: Boxing grew from bare-knuckle street and military survival manuals (Dempsey's Championship Fighting) into the most pressure-tested stand-up system on earth. Tyson and Cus forged that lineage in gyms where getting hit was mandatory — not cooperative demos. That training culture is why boxing crosses rank #1 while karate gyaku-zuki shares lab newtons but fails delivery proof.
historicalContext.note: Biographical street-fight counts (Tyson 100+ as kid) are anecdotal — not NCVS outcomes

legendAnalysis.coreTeaching: Everybody has a plan until punched in the mouth; peek-a-boo swarming; slip haymaker → counter bomb; overwhelming violence of action
aligns: 64% KOs in first 10 sec — script disruption matches burst window
Slip + counter = boxing jab/cross Tier A under resistance
Psychological collapse of untrained attackers after clean first strike (Tier B inference)
Close-range head movement vs telegraphed street hooks
tensions: Peek-a-boo forward pressure risks entanglement with multiples or hidden weapons
Tyson's street anecdotes are Tier C/D — no victimization RCT
'Hit first' framing conflicts with legal de-escalation and EAAA avoidance-first
legendAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Tyson is fight-science burst personified. Borrow slips, counters, and first-strike commitment — not prolonged swarming. Stun, don't become a stationary target.

legendAnalysis.coreTeaching: Hero and coward feel same fear — channel it; make them miss and pay; unseen punch breaks will; relentless pressure not running
aligns: Fear control supports AAA habit consistency under stress
Hit-and-not-get-hit via head movement — Tier A delivery culture
Deception + timing = action beats reaction at punch range (A4)
Cus lost eye in street fight — understood real violence
tensions: 'Relentless pressure' ≠ Resilience escape-priority when outnumbered
Psychology claims lack peer-reviewed civilian SD trials
legendAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Cus supplies the mental operating system boxing proves physically. Pair his aggression with Funakoshi's exit: pressure to create the opening, sprint to use it.

Aggressive defense = simultaneous block-counter Motobu logic
Short power shots over wild swings — matches street footage
Civilian/military SD manuals — practical not sport-only
tensions: 1970s mugger KO anecdotes are Tier C hero stories
No modern RCT on Dempsey-method civilian outcomes
legendAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Dempsey is the bridge between boxing science and self-defense writing. His power mechanics are Tier A biomechanics; his 'finish fast' maps to D3 ten-second burst.

legendAnalysis.coreTeaching: Footwork, angles, distance — make them miss while creating escape lanes
aligns: A3 pre-contact leverage via angle not duel
Footwork creates exit routes — matches defensivePosture footwork rules
Jab range control ranks #3 technique with streetSafety 5
tensions: Ali's style assumes ring space and referee — street has obstacles and third parties
Dancing prolongs exposure vs sprint exit
legendAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Ali is the boxing argument for 'don't stand and trade — move off the X.' Best legend alignment with escape-priority strategy.

legendAnalysis.coreTeaching: Shoulder roll, slip without over-commit; counter off defense
aligns: Head movement and shoulder roll reduce clean chin shots
Philly shell high guard overlaps compact exit-ready guard
tensions: Shell defense invites clinch and ground in street — 62–73% fights hit ground
Mayweather career = prolonged defense — opposite of ikken hissatsu + run
Sport reflexes need years — violates A7 for casual civilians
legendAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Borrow the slip mechanics, reject the 'pillow-fight for 12 rounds' mindset. One slip → one counter → exit.

limitations.title: What legends admit (and data confirms)
items: 1v1 stand-up assumption — groups and weapons change everything
No ground game — 62–73% of fights hit ground; boxing default is dangerous there
No kick defense — karate/Muay teep and calf kick fill the gap
Legal exposure — 'hit first' requires imminent threat standard; EAAA de-escalation first
Hand injury risk on bare concrete — palm heel option from karate stack
Sport rounds mindset vs 10-second burst — train exit not decision victory
limitations.hybridNote: Boxing refines the straight punch and head movement karate needs. Karate adds low kicks, palms, and Okinawan exit philosophy. Adversarial optimal stack: EAAA brain + boxing hands + wrestling sprawl + explicit run conditioning.

Jab to measure/disrupt — power only if they commit
Target body to fold them; head for decisive stun

drillProgression.title: 8-Week Boxing Survival Progression (Stun-and-Run)
drillProgression.prerequisite: AAA awareness running; legal understanding of proportional response

drills: High guard shadow: chin tucked, elbows in — 3×2 min
Jab-only shadow: touch guard, recover, never overreach — 100 jabs
Fence drill: partner steps in verbally — jab to chest guard line, angle step off
phases.exitTest: Maintain guard 2 min continuous shadow; 20 clean jabs in 30 sec

drills: Falling step cross on heavy bag — sit into punch, exhale — 5×20
Short hook on bag: elbow level, rotate through target — 3×15/side
Body shot ladder: solar plexus → liver line on bag — 3×10 each
phases.exitTest: 10 power crosses in 20 sec with hip drive; partner feels effective mass

drills: Slip outside lead hook → cross counter on mitts — 5×10
Peek-a-boo burst: slip-slip-hook-cross on bag — 3×1 min (max 4 punches)
Haymaker feed: partner throws slow overhand — slip and counter once only
phases.exitTest: Counter within one beat of feed; mandatory verbal RUN after combo

drills: Jab-cross-hook burst → teep push (borrowed from karate stack) → 10 m sprint
Clinch: frame → elbow or knee once → break and run
Multi-angle: attacker from side — Ali pivot + cross + exit
phases.exitTest: Complete burst-exit in under 3 sec on surprise cue; never stand after third punch

drillProgression.maintenance: 1 light spar/month + weekly bag power + daily 3 min awareness. Re-test exit sprint monthly.

boxingNhiAnalysis.nhiFinalVerdict: The boxing angle to self-defense is the strongest contact-phase case on this site. Tyson, Cus, and Dempsey independently discovered what our data measures: delivery under resistance beats technique collection, the first ten seconds decide standing fights, and wild untrained swings lose to slips and counters. Ali adds what Tyson often forgot — angles are escape routes, not just dominance. Resilience adds four corrections: (1) awareness still outranks every punch; (2) forward swarming without exit is a liability against multiples and weapons; (3) boxing must borrow karate's teep and wrestling's sprawl to cover kicks and ground; (4) reject 'dominate most encounters' unless someone runs the victimization study. Train boxing hands with Cus's mindset, Ali's feet, Dempsey's mass — then Funakoshi's run.
boxingNhiAnalysis.honestLimits: Legend quotes and street-fight anecdotes are Tier C/D. No RCT isolates 'boxers' vs 'non-boxers' for civilian assault victimization. Tyson's peek-a-boo and Mayweather's shell are sport optimizations — street extrapolation is inference. This analysis reconciles philosophy with existing Resilience tiers; it does not claim boxing reduces crime rates without measured outcomes.

kickingNhiAnalysis.title: Kicking Experts vs Resilience Evidence — AI NHI Reconciliation
kickingNhiAnalysis.executiveSummary: Kicking authorities across Muay Thai, kickboxing, Kyokushin, and practical karate converge on what Resilience kickingTechniques already ranks: low-to-mid kicks and teeps dominate street survival; high flashy head kicks are sport finishers, not primary weapons. Calf kick ranks #1 (streetSafety 5), teep #2 (escape-priority Tier A), low roundhouse #3. Wallace, Rutten, Buakaw/Saenchai, Oyama, Lewis, and Abernethy independently reject head-kick-first street doctrine. Resilience adds: (1) awareness still outranks every kick; (2) Wallace's elite high kicks are an exception proving the rule — speed + setup, not civilian default; (3) kicks manage range and mobility destruction — boxing still owns close chaos; (4) ground risk (62–73%) means kicking then running beats kicking then trading.

historicalContext.plainEnglish: Legs reach farther than arms and hit harder per square inch when conditioned — but street surfaces, shoes, and grab risk punish reckless chambers. Muay Thai weaponized the teep and low roundhouse in full contact; Wallace proved speed can make high kicks work for elites; Oyama proved low kicks end fights after brutal conditioning. The expert consensus is selection discipline, not kick collection.
historicalContext.note: Expert interviews and pedagogy — no civilian kick-only victimization RCT

expertAnalysis.coreTeaching: Fast, accurate, set-up kicks; sideways protected stance; instant retraction; low/mid safer for most; high kicks only with elite speed
aligns: Setup + retraction = stun-and-escape burst logic
Low/mid kicks safer for civilians — matches streetSafety rankings
Fake/deception supports jab-feint → kick combos
Sideways blade minimizes target profile — defensivePosture 45°
tensions: Wallace's high kicks are elite-athlete exception — roundhouse-head streetSafety 2
Kickboxing ruleset experience ≠ weapon/multi-attacker street
One-legged specialist after injury — not generalizable body type
expertAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Wallace validates setup discipline and retraction speed. Civilians should train his low-line system, not his highlight-reel head kicks unless they have his speed under C6 testing.

expertAnalysis.coreTeaching: Low kicks destroy mobility; teep #1 for distance; oblique kick to thigh/calf; knees in clinch; avoid high kicks unless elite + stunned opponent
aligns: Calf/low roundhouse rank #1-3 kicks — Tier A
Teep = escape-priority create-distance tactic
Leg destruction prevents pursuit — D2 escape science
Clinch knee pairs with boxing dirty boxing section
tensions: Rutten SD programs are Tier C pedagogy without victimization RCT
MMA-era leg kicks assume sport base — concrete + shoes change balance
expertAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Rutten is the clearest voice aligning Muay Thai leg logic with Resilience data. His teep + low kick stack is the kicking half of the boxing+karate hybrid.

expertAnalysis.coreTeaching: Teep controls range; low roundhouse to thigh/calf cripples movement; shin conditioning; high kicks only as finishers after setup
aligns: Teep most important SD kick — matches rank #2 + escape stack
Shin kicks to leg — 3000N roundhouse lab data, fightValidated
High kick as finisher only — confirms roundhouse-head Tier B not primary
Conditioning culture supports C6 stress transfer
tensions: Stadium fighters — decades of daily shin conditioning not replicable in 8 weeks
Thai clinch culture risks ground entanglement on street
expertAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Muay Thai kings confirm the Resilience kick leaderboard. Borrow teep and low roundhouse; borrow conditioning mindset; skip prolonged clinch without exit.

expertAnalysis.coreTeaching: Low/mid kicks after extreme conditioning; ikken hissatsu one-kick finish; low roundhouse and front kick to legs/body
aligns: Oyama low kick = calf kick evidence in adversarial + technique rank
Ikken hissatsu kick = first 10 sec burst
Full-contact knockdown = C6 validation culture
Body/leg targeting over head — streetSafety alignment
tensions: Knockdown tournaments ≠ weapons/multiples
Body hardening years — A7 finite training time tension for casual civilians
expertAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Oyama is the karate bridge to Rutten/Buakaw low-kick consensus. Same kick, different conditioning timeline.

expertAnalysis.coreTeaching: Integrated kicks with boxing hands; low/mid kicks for practical power; full-contact pioneer
aligns: Hands + kicks combo — jab + low kick in adversarial optimal stack
Full-contact delivery culture — C6
Low/mid power generation over flash
tensions: Historical full-contact karate — limited modern street outcome data
expertAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Lewis predicted the Resilience synthesis stack decades early: boxing range chaos + low kick mobility kill.

expertAnalysis.coreTeaching: Kicks in context after hand distraction; low-line kata applications; pressure-tested bunkai
aligns: Hand distract → kick = confirmed combo pattern
Low-line bunkai matches teep/calf/groin kick tiers
C6 pressure testing requirement — same as karate NHI
tensions: Kata-derived kicks without resistance = traditional MA rank 7 problem
Context-dependent — not standalone kick-first
expertAnalysis.nhiVerdict: Abernethy adds the when-not just the what: kicks follow hands or create distance — never open naked on a fresh opponent if avoidable.

drillProgression.title: 8-Week Kicking Survival Progression (Low-Line First)
drillProgression.prerequisite: AAA awareness; basic boxing guard from boxing NHI stack

drills: Teep to pad: hip thrust, ball of foot, full retraction — 3×20 each leg
Push-step teep: kick → land in sprint stance → shadow 5 m exit
Wall teep for balance: light contact, recover guard under 1 sec
phases.exitTest: 15 teeps in 30 sec with guard recovery; verbal RUN after each

drills: Shin-to-bag low roundhouse: chop calf line — 5×15 each leg
Oblique kick to thigh on pad — 3×10 (Bas-style)
Partner hold pad: one leg kick only — retract instantly
phases.exitTest: 10 low kicks in 20 sec without crossing feet or falling

drills: Jab feint → calf kick → exit angle step
Cross distraction → teep push → 10 m sprint
Groin snap kick (mae-geri) only after hand frame — 5×10
phases.exitTest: Land hand-kick combo in one beat; mandatory exit after kick

drills: Attacker rush: side teep or knee-line stop → run
Clinch entry: one knee → break frame → teep → exit
Multi-attacker awareness: one kick max then sprint — no second kick
phases.exitTest: Surprise feed → one low-line kick → exit under 3 sec

drillProgression.maintenance: Weekly Thai pads + daily teep shadow + shin conditioning 2×/week. No head kicks in street scenarios unless advanced C6.

kickingNhiAnalysis.nhiFinalVerdict: The kicking expert consensus is one of the cleanest alignments on Resilience: teep for escape, calf/low roundhouse for mobility destruction, hands to set up, instant retraction, and high kicks demoted to elite finisher status. Wallace proves speed can bend the rule — Rutten, Buakaw, Oyama, and Abernethy prove the rule itself. Resilience adds: awareness still layer zero; one kick then run beats kick exchanges; boxing owns punch range, kicks own leg reach; wrestling sprawl still required for ground risk. Train Rutten's teep, Buakaw's calf line, Lewis's jab-kick, Oyama's commitment — with Funakoshi's exit and Tyson's burst hands.
kickingNhiAnalysis.honestLimits: Expert interviews and training videos are Tier C pedagogy. No RCT measures 'kick-trained civilians' vs others for assault victimization. Wallace high-kick exception is n=1 elite athlete. Shin conditioning timelines are years in Muay Thai/Kyokushin — not 8-week shortcuts. This reconciles expert consensus with existing technique tiers; it does not elevate kicking above EAAA avoidance.
